verses.text: Samuel grows old and makes his sons judges, but they 'turned aside after lucre,' took bribes, and perverted justice. Even a godly man's children may go astray. Office is no guarantee of integrity; each generation must choose the LORD for itself.

Samuel grows old and makes his sons judges, but they 'turned aside after lucre,' took bribes, and perverted justice. Even a godly man's children may go astray. Office is no guarantee of integrity; each generation must choose the LORD for itself.

Samuel grows old and makes his sons judges, but they 'turned aside after lucre,' took bribes, and perverted justice. Even a godly man's children may go astray. Office is no guarantee of integrity; each generation must choose the LORD for itself.

The elders come asking for a king 'like all the nations.' Their complaint about the corrupt sons is fair, but their cure is wrong: they want to be like everyone else. The deepest danger is not bad leaders but the craving to look like the world around us.

The elders come asking for a king 'like all the nations.' Their complaint about the corrupt sons is fair, but their cure is wrong: they want to be like everyone else. The deepest danger is not bad leaders but the craving to look like the world around us.

Samuel is grieved, but God tells him the truth: 'they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me, that I should not reign over them.' To want a visible king instead of the unseen LORD was to refuse God's own kingship. He grants it — but warns them first.

Samuel is grieved, but God tells him the truth: 'they have not rejected thee, but they have rejected me, that I should not reign over them.' To want a visible king instead of the unseen LORD was to refuse God's own kingship. He grants it — but warns them first.

Samuel spells out 'the manner of the king': he will take their sons for his chariots and wars, their daughters for his kitchens, their best young men for his service. The king they crave to serve them will end up making servants of them. Worldly power always takes.

Samuel spells out 'the manner of the king': he will take their sons for his chariots and wars, their daughters for his kitchens, their best young men for his service. The king they crave to serve them will end up making servants of them. Worldly power always takes.

He will seize their fields and harvests and a tenth of all, until 'ye shall be his servants' and cry out — but the LORD will not hear them then. What we demand against God's will, He may grant as a sorrow. Be careful what you insist upon having.

He will seize their fields and harvests and a tenth of all, until 'ye shall be his servants' and cry out — but the LORD will not hear them then. What we demand against God's will, He may grant as a sorrow. Be careful what you insist upon having.

Yet the people refuse to listen: 'Nay; but we will have a king,' to be like the nations and to fight their battles. God says, 'make them a king.' Sometimes the heaviest judgment is simply to be given exactly what we stubbornly demand.
